full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One of the really fascinating things about human beings, about all of us, is that we remember.<|10.34|> <|10.34|> We remember things, we remember people, and we remember all of it in very powerful and vivid ways.<|19.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And, just as much as we remember, we try to look ahead into the future and predict it<|7.34|> <|7.34|> or control it or get ready for it.<|9.56|> <|9.56|> We make plans and all that.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> But whether we're looking back or looking forward, we do all of it in the present moment.<|20.04|> <|20.04|> Little wonder then that we're sometimes neurotic.<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The most emotional part of all souls is certainly the remembering and the music.<|8.08|> <|8.96|> We read the names of all souls, people who would not be on any official list of saints,<|15.58|> <|15.94|> but souls that are near and dear to us that made all the difference in our own lives.<|22.12|> <|23.00|> And it's fascinating to me that we remember in such vivid detail that<|29.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For example, I remember my maternal grandmother, Mary Fuller.<|5.20|> <|5.20|> I remember her hugs, her stories, her humor, and her cigarettes.<|14.44|> <|14.44|> Her living room was as smoky as this cathedral.<|19.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.74|> I finally got you to laugh, by the way.<|4.28|> <|5.54|> I thought I had a funny line earlier about being neurotic, but it didn't land well out there in the nave.<|11.68|> <|14.26|> I remember my parish priest, John Cruz, of blessed memory.<|18.94|> <|18.94|> He said,<|19.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> the priest who sent me to seminary. I see him right now in his office. He was always just<|7.44|> <|7.44|> surrounded by books. He had bookshelves just filled with books and stacks of books<|13.44|> <|14.00|> on his desk. And he always had a mischievous grin on his face and a cup of black coffee<|20.96|> <|21.52|> in his hand at any hour. I don't know how he ever slept.<|27.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> By the mid-2nd century, or at the latest, the early 3rd century of Christianity, that<|6.62|> <|6.62|> is 1,800 years ago, give or take, Christians started gathering locally at the tomb of a<|16.12|> <|16.12|> martyr on the anniversary of that person's death.<|20.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> They went to the tomb on that anniversary, they said prayers,<|4.64|> <|4.64|> they told stories about the highlights of that person's life,<|9.46|> <|9.46|> and they often celebrated the Eucharist there on the tomb,<|13.60|> <|13.60|> which became an altar table,<|15.86|> <|15.86|> and even occasionally and dramatically poured wine into the tomb of their loved one as a way of communing with them.<|23.88|> <|23.88|> How's that for a vivid image for the communion of saints?<|29.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In this way, a burial ground, human memories, ordinary human memories,<|5.70|> <|6.44|> and the Eucharist all come together as a sacrament that is outward and visible signs<|11.26|> <|11.26|> of inward and spiritual grace.<|13.70|> <|15.36|> Therefore, what we do on this night with our sacred burial ground just outside that eastern wall is<|23.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> named to all souls, as we read these names, say our prayers, and celebrate the Eucharist,<|7.94|> <|7.96|> what we're doing tonight is something that's been done for almost 1,800 years,<|14.76|> <|15.32|> which means we're not making this up.<|18.92|> <|20.04|> It's a tradition that's passed hand to hand, just like bread and heart to heart.<|25.42|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> there.<|9.04|> <|9.04|> His name was Norman Pittenger.<|11.42|> <|11.42|> He was a famous theologian.<|13.22|> <|13.22|> I don't know if theologians are famous.<|14.84|> <|14.84|> He was famous at our seminary.<|18.32|> <|18.32|> He loved the liturgy.<|19.82|> <|19.82|> He loved prayers like these tonight.<|22.74|> <|22.74|> He loved prayers for the departed.<|24.68|> <|24.68|> He was more high church.<|26.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But he was also very much so a kind of mid-century rationalist and wanted to do the best he could<|7.38|> <|7.38|> to think through what the Christian faith means and try in a way to put it in a way that's<|15.10|> <|15.10|> understandable and relatable, consistent with the Bible but with some fresh images.<|19.32|> <|20.46|> And one of the conclusions that he reached about the meaning of resurrection I find profoundly<|28.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> profoundly comforting. Resurrection, he wrote, can be imagined in part, in part,<|9.68|> <|9.68|> as God remembering us. Resurrection, Pittenger writes, is in part God bringing<|18.36|> <|18.36|> the past into the immediacy of the present divine awareness from which<|23.50|> <|23.50|> nothing can be lost.<|26.70|> memory is a lot better than ours, of course,<|14.82|> <|15.88|> especially in the way that the divine memory in Christ<|18.66|> <|18.66|> holds and heals the memories of people in a way<|22.72|> forever.<|12.76|> <|12.76|> But I want to end where I started with our ability as human beings not just to remember<|19.72|> <|19.72|> and look back, although that's lovely sometimes, but our ability to plan and hope for a better<|26.30|> <|26.30|> future.<|27.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Frederick Buechner, a great writer and Presbyterian minister who died in the last great journey.<|18.28|> <|18.28|> You might remember a couple of these prayers, that the departed goes from strength to strength<|23.92|> <|23.92|> in a new life of perfect service.<|26.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> or that the departed is ever increasing in the knowledge of Thee, O God.<|6.42|> <|7.84|> In these prayers, the emphasis is on the soul making a great journey into God's presence in ways we can't even fathom.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In other words, the emphasis is on the future, not the past.<|5.48|> <|5.48|> But here's why this matters.<|8.50|> <|8.50|> Buechner then concludes, and be patient with me as I read, please.<|14.04|> <|14.04|> Buechner concludes, "One imagines all of us on this shore fading for them the departed<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> as they journey ahead toward whatever new shore may await them.<|5.56|> <|6.56|> That is perhaps why to think of them as a matter<|9.24|> <|9.24|> not only of remembering them as they used to be,<|13.10|> <|14.22|> but of seeing and hearing them as in some sense they are now.<|19.44|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> -<|1.76|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.08|> At this all-souls requiem then, let us not only remember our loved ones,<|9.80|> <|10.22|> but leave plenty of room for imagination.<|13.40|> <|14.10|> That is, for all of the countless and unimaginable ways that in God's presence<|20.12|> <|20.12|> they have become even more of who God wants them to be.<|25.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
